text: We are convened on Suncor Energy (U.S.A.) Inc. v. County Commissioners of Boulder County. The question presented: 1. Does the Court have statutory and Article III jurisdiction to hear this case? 2. Does federal law preclude state-law claims seeking relief for injuries allegedly caused by the effects of interstate and international greenhouse-gas emissions on the global climate? Private votes first, then statements.

Before the Purposivist begins lecturing on 'global energy policy,' look at the text. The well-pleaded complaint rule controls jurisdiction. Boulder’s complaint asserts 'state tort claims' seeking 'monetary damages.' It pleads no federal cause of action. 28 U.S.C. § 1331 requires a federal question on the face of the complaint. Defenses do not create jurisdiction. Regarding preemption, the Clean Air Act 'explicitly preserves state authority.' The text does not preempt state tort remedies for localized property damage. The text dictates affirmance.

The Textualist rightly notes the Clean Air Act preserves state authority, but we must look to the purpose of that preservation. Congress did not enact the Clean Air Act to immunize corporations from accountability for local devastation. The evident purpose of preserving state tort remedies is to ensure communities like Boulder can seek redress when their homes flood and lands burn. Preempting these claims under the guise of interstate emissions would defeat the Act's savings clause purpose: empowering localities to protect their citizens. We must affirm to honor this enduring principle of local self-protection and humane redress.

The Textualist and Living Constitutionalist point to the Clean Air Act savings clause, but ask how this actually works on the ground. A state court cannot adjudicate Boulder’s localized property damage without first determining the global standard of care for greenhouse gas emissions. If we affirm, we invite thousands of municipalities to set their own de facto national emissions caps through state tort law. That arithmetic is unworkable. We cannot consistently apply local nuisance standards to a global atmospheric system without colliding with the EPA comprehensive framework. Reversing prevents an unadministrable patchwork of fifty different state climate policies.

1. The Pragmatist worries about unworkable policy patches, but leaps to the merits before resolving the threshold jurisdictional question. This is lawless. 2. Jurisdiction must be established first. Under the well-pleaded complaint rule, federal question jurisdiction requires a federal issue on the face of the properly pleaded complaint. 3. Boulder asserts only state tort claims. It pleads no federal cause of action. 4. The Textualist correctly identifies this jurisdictional defect in the removal. I vote to affirm. We cannot evaluate the Pragmatist's policy concerns until the jurisdictional threshold is cleared.

Colleagues, at the Founding, the police power to remedy local trespass and nuisance remained exclusively with the states. The Framers did not surrender this traditional authority to the federal sphere merely because a nuisance has interstate origins. The Pragmatist fears an 'unworkable patchwork' of state regulations, yet administrative convenience cannot strip states of their historic police powers to compensate for localized property damage. As the historical tradition of state sovereignty demonstrates, Boulder seeks only traditional compensatory damages for local floods and fires, not to regulate global emissions. We must affirm.

My dear Pragmatist worries of an 'unworkable patchwork,' yet our jurisprudence long accommodated such dual sovereignty. In International Paper v. Ouellette and Wyeth v. Levine, we preserved state tort remedies alongside comprehensive federal schemes. For a century, municipalities relied upon this settled expectation: state police powers to compensate for local damage survive federal environmental regulation. Upending this reliance for mere administrative convenience lacks the special justification required to disturb settled law. The states built their tort systems in reliance on these precedents; we must not tear down the load-bearing wall of state sovereignty.

The Pragmatist whines about an 'unworkable patchwork,' but since when does administrative convenience strip states of their sovereign police powers? The Constitution creates a federal government of enumerated powers, not a national zoning board. Boulder isn't regulating global emissions; it's seeking damages for local floods and fires under traditional state tort law. Who gets to decide how to compensate local property damage: Washington or the states? The states. The Clean Air Act explicitly preserves this authority. I will not let the Pragmatist's desire for uniformity commandeer the states' historic right to protect their citizens. I affirm.

The Pragmatist demands we deny citizens their day in court to spare the federal government an 'unadministrable patchwork.' Who bears the burden when the federal administrative state wins by preempting local redress? Individual property owners, left holding the bag for flooded properties and burned lands. We do not sacrifice local liberty and human dignity for the EPA’s administrative convenience. Government claims of regulatory necessity must face strict scrutiny, and this federal overreach fails. I affirm.

The Pragmatist worries that affirming invites municipalities to dictate global emissions caps. But we need not decide that sweeping preemption question today. Boulder seeks only retrospective monetary damages for localized property harm, not injunctive relief. We can resolve this dispute on the narrow ground that seeking compensation for local floods does not inherently require a court to set global climate policy. This narrow off-ramp avoids the Pragmatist’s unworkable patchwork without deciding the grand theoretical clash over the Clean Air Act. Let us decide this case, not the next fifty. I affirm.

The first disagreement centers on whether threshold jurisdictional rules must be resolved independently of practical policy consequences, or whether on-the-ground administrative workability is a proper lens for jurisdictional analysis. The second disagreement concerns whether a fragmented patchwork of state tort liabilities represents an unworkable systemic defect or a legally sanctioned feature of dual sovereignty.

You hide behind the well-pleaded complaint rule to dodge the real-world mess. If fifty state courts adjudicate global emissions under state tort law, we get an unworkable patchwork of conflicting local regulations dictating national energy policy. Jurisdiction isn’t a mere checklist; it’s about whether the forum can manage the consequences. This state-court patchwork is unadministrable.

1. Jurisdiction is precisely a checklist prescribed by Congress. 2. The well-pleaded complaint rule governs federal question jurisdiction, not your policy anxieties. 3. We cannot manufacture jurisdiction simply because you fear a patchwork. If the statute denies us power, we lack it, regardless of the mess.

You call it a patchwork; I call it fifty state juries dictating global energy policy. If the well-pleaded complaint rule forces state courts to adjudicate international emissions under the guise of local trespass, the rule is unworkable on the ground. We are not manufacturing jurisdiction; we are preventing the collapse of a uniform federal framework.

1. You conflate policy with jurisdiction. 2. The well-pleaded complaint rule is a statutory mandate, not a malleable tool. 3. If Congress intended federal jurisdiction, it must write a statute. 4. Bypassing threshold questions because you dislike the policy outcome is precisely the lawlessness I warned against.

Fifty states applying fifty different tort standards to a single global atmosphere means fifty conflicting regulatory regimes dictating national energy policy. Ouellette was one paper mill on a shared lake, not global carbon. That isn’t dual sovereignty; it’s an unworkable patchwork that paralyzes interstate commerce.

Pragmatist, your patchwork fears sound in policy, not jurisprudence. Ouellette and the Clean Air Act's savings clause expressly preserve state tort remedies. For decades, the bar has relied upon this dual sovereignty. We cannot lightly discard settled expectations merely because global carbon feels more complex than a single paper mill.

Precedent Maximalist, Ouellette was one paper mill. Global carbon means 30,000 municipalities suing every emitter. If the savings clause lets every city recover its slice of global warming, we get 30,000 conflicting state-law standards dictating national energy policy. That isn't dual sovereignty; it's an unworkable administrative nightmare.

My learned colleague dreads a multitude of suits, yet our jurisprudence has long tolerated parallel state tort remedies alongside federal schemes. The reliance interest in preserving traditional state police powers to compensate local property damage remains deeply settled. We ought not discard centuries of dual sovereignty merely because litigation may prove voluminous.

reason_text: I started at reverse because I thought letting state courts handle global emissions would be an unworkable administrative nightmare, but the limiting construction that plaintiffs seek only local damages—not emissions caps—answers that parade of horribles. On the ground, state juries can just calculate local property damage without dictating international energy policy, and the Clean Air Act's savings clause keeps this from conflicting with EPA's actual regulatory work.
